oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Treatment options for lameness in beef cattle
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Nelson Warr, Brian
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: bovine
lameness
feedlot
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Lameness remains a significant concern in beef cattle pro­duction but has received limited attention in research and published work. Accurate diagnosis is the starting point for effective lameness treatment protocols. The majority of cattle lameness occurs in the foot. It is imperative that veterinar­ians encourage producers to pick up the feet of lame cattle and differentiate common causes of foot lameness. Footrot treat­ment is focused on parenteral antimicrobials and there is pub­lished evidence for ceftiofur sodium, ceftiofur hydrochloride, ceftiofur crystalline free acid, florfenicol, tulathromycin and oxytetracycline. Papillomatous digital dermatitis treatment in beef cattle usually focuses on topical treatment options con­taining copper. Footbaths have been utilized in some feedlots but the production practices in beef cattle limit frequency of trips through the footbath. Septic arthritis treatment is based on selection of products with efficacy for Mycoplasma bovis, but no products are labeled for this condition. Toe tip necrosis treatment usually consists of “tipping” the toe and administer­ing antimicrobials for secondary conditions. Laminitis has no practical treatment, but it is important for the veterinarian to work with producers to market laminitis cases early. Finally, injury cases are usually treated with non-steroidal anti-inflam­matory drugs.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; No. 1 (2026): 2026 Recent Graduate Conference Proceedings; 25-27
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
